content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on the array, as long as it has more than one element:


Choose any two **adjacent** elements, remove them from the array and insert their sum at that position.
Formally, if the current length of the array is ∣A∣|A|∣A∣, you can choose an index 1≤i<∣A∣1 \leq i \lt |A|1≤i<∣A∣, and transform the array into [A1,A2,…,Ai−1,Ai+Ai+1,Ai+2,…,AN][A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{i-1}, A_i + A_{i+1}, A_{i+2}, \ldots, A_N][A1​,A2​,…,Ai−1​,Ai​+Ai+1​,Ai+2​,…,AN​].
Note that after each operation, the length of array decreases by exactly 111.

Print the minimum number of operations to be applied on array AAA such that all the elements in the resulting array are equal. See sample explanation for more details.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line contains an integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, the elements of array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations required to make all the elements equal.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^4
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the minimum number of operations required to make all elements of the array equal.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: total sum = 10. The best partition is [5,5] (target 5) → 2 segments, operations = 3‑2 = 1.
Test case 2: sum = 15, can keep whole array as one segment (k=1) → operations = 2‑1 = 1.
Test case 3: sum = 27, best partition into 3 segments of sum 9 → operations = 5‑3 = 2.
Test case 4: all elements already equal, sum = 9, partition into 3 segments of sum 3 → operations = 3‑3 = 0.
Total sum is 0, we can keep each element separate, so no operation is needed.
Sum = -10. The whole array already consists of equal elements, so k = 5 and operations = 5‑5 = 0.

solution.approach: Let S be the total sum of the array. After all operations the array will consist of k equal elements, each equal to S/k, therefore k must divide S. The operation count equals N‑k, so we want the largest possible k (the most segments) such that the array can be split into k contiguous parts each having sum S/k. For a fixed k we scan the array accumulating a running sum. Whenever the running sum equals the required part sum we start a new part; if it ever exceeds the required sum (or becomes smaller when the required sum is negative) the partition is impossible. We test possible k in decreasing order (from N down to 1) and stop at the first feasible k. The special case S = 0 is easy: we can keep every element as a separate part, so answer is 0. The algorithm runs in O(N·d) where d is the number of divisors of |S| (at most about 6·10^4 for the given limits), well within the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N · number_of_divisors(|S|)) per test case, ≤ O(N·√|S|). With total N ≤ 3·10^4 this easily fits in time limits.
